text: We present a robotic-assisted pylorus sparing pancreatic duodenectomy. The patient is a seventy-six-year-old male who presented with epigastric pain and jaundice this past November. His medical history is listed below. His preoperative workup includes an ERCP, which demonstrated an ulcerative infiltrative mass at the major papilla. This was biopsied and came back positive for invasive adenocarcinoma. On preop imaging, we demonstrate the ulcerative mass on the left and extra and intrapatic biliary dilation on the right.
The video shows the port placement for a robotic-assisted laparoscopic pylorus-preserving Whipple procedure.

The surgeon begins dissection along the greater curvature of the stomach to access the lesser sac. Omental and peritoneal adhesions are carefully dissected from the liver and gallbladder. The dissection proceeds through the hepatocolic ligament to mobilize the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon mobilizes the colon and continues cocorization, reflecting the duodenum medially. Superior duodenal attachments to the ligament of Treitz are removed, followed by careful removal of retrogastric adhesions.
The surgeon isolates the gastroepiploic vein and divides it using Weck clips. Dissection proceeds through the hepatoduodenal ligament, carefully separating the tissue.
The surgeon creates a retroduodenal tunnel and transects the duodenum using a 60mm linear stapler. The gastroepiploic arcade is then divided with a vascular load.
The surgeon isolates the gastroduodenal artery and divides it between Weck clips. Dissection proceeds to skeletonize the common hepatic duct, revealing its structure and surrounding tissue.
The surgeon frees the gallbladder using a top-down approach, encircles the common hepatic duct with umbilical tape, and divides the pancreas at the neck using hook cautery.

The surgeon further divides the ligament of Treitz, enabling retraction of the jejunum into the retroportal space. The uncinate process is dissected free from the superior mesenteric artery and vein using a vessel sealer, visibly separating these structures.
The surgeon sharply divides the common hepatic duct. The proximal jejunum is transected using a sixty millimeter linear stapler. The pancreatic duct is cannulated with an eight French feeding tube.
The surgeon performs an end-to-side pancreaticojejunostomy, using a 3-0 suture for the posterior and anterior layers and a 5-0 suture for the duct-to-mucosa inner layer.

The surgeon performs an end-to-side hepatico-jejunostomy using a single interrupted 4-0 PDS suture layer. The jejunum is brought in an anticholic fashion, followed by the creation of an end-to-side duodenal jejunostomy using a running 3-0 suture layer.

At the conclusion of the pancreaticoduodenectomy, two 19 French Blake drains are placed, one adjacent to the hepatico-jejunostomy and the other near the duodenal jejunostomy. The assist port is slightly enlarged to facilitate the removal of the surgical specimen.
Thank you for sharing our experience with a totally robotic pancreatic duodenectomy.
